title: The Ghost Warden of Glimthaven
subtitle: What do you owe the dead?

player.name: The Warden
player.backstory: You were left behind. When the Bastard's war ended and the survivors marched south to claim their inheritance, someone had to stay and guard what the keep contained — the old names carved in stone, the treaty-seals, the bones beneath the floor. That someone was you. Seven winters have passed. No one has come back. But the gods have started visiting.

prologue:
The sea does not forgive. It does not remember, either — which is worse. The cliffs below Glimthaven Keep have swallowed three fishing boats this season alone, and the keep itself offers nothing but a torch in a high window and the sound of your own footsteps on wet stone.

You were not born a warden. You were made one by absence. The Bastard is gone south, the Iron Company disbanded, the Silver Court assembled its treaties in marble rooms that have never smelled of salt. The green forests grew back over old battle-lines and forgot the names of the fallen. And you — you stayed.

Not out of duty. Not out of fear. You stayed because someone has to remember. The names are cut into the stones below the hall. The seals of the old compact are locked in the chest you sleep beside. The dead are not gone — they are simply quiet, and they require a witness.

Tonight the storm has broken something loose. A presence walks the battlements that has no footsteps. The torch in the high window has begun to burn a color that fire should not be. And in the great hall, where the names of the dead are carved deepest, a voice has begun to speak — not in any mortal tongue, but in the language that gods use when they have decided to notice you.

You have been faithful. You have been forgotten. Now something divine is hungry for what you've kept.

What will you give it? And at what cost?

endings.authority: They call you the Warden-King now, though you never claimed the title. The gods recognized something in you that the living overlooked: you held the keep when everyone else left, which means the keep is yours by the oldest law — the law of witness. You opened the chest. You read the names aloud to the storm. You claimed the compact in your own voice and dared any power in heaven or earth to dispute it. None did. The torches in Glimthaven Keep burn gold now, not gray. Messengers have started arriving from the south — tentative, a little frightened. They can feel the weight of something reestablished here, some authority reconstituted from grief and stubbornness. You did not set out to rule. But you refused to be forgotten, which amounts to the same thing.
endings.wisdom: The gods did not take the compact. They translated it. You spent the long nights of visitation not defending the chest or swearing oaths but asking questions — what the seals meant in the original tongue, what the names of the dead had been before the war renamed them, what the keep itself was built to guard before anyone remembered the reason. The answers came slowly, and they cost you: sleep, certainty, the comfortable belief that the past was settled. But you know now what Glimthaven Keep truly is — not a fortress but a threshold, not a grave but a gate. The dead do not haunt it. They wait in it. And now that you understand the mechanism, you can decide what comes through, and what does not.
endings.courage: The gods came to take. You stood in the doorway of the great hall and told them no. This is the part of the story that the songs will get wrong — they'll say you were fearless, that you drove the divine away with iron will. The truth is you were terrified every night of those six visitations, and you stood your ground anyway, which is the only kind of courage that matters. The compact remains sealed. The names remain your stewardship. The keep stands. When word reached the south that something had tried to breach Glimthaven and been refused, the messengers came not with demands but with respect. You have become a landmark — not a king, not a scholar, but a fixed point that the world now navigates around.
endings.cunning: You gave the gods what they asked for. Every one of them. The trick was that what they asked for and what they actually wanted were never the same thing. Zeus wanted the authority of the compact — you gave him a copy, kept the original, and let him believe the ink was the same as the stone. Hermes wanted the names — you gave him the names of the dead's horses, which horses remember better than men anyway. By the sixth visitation, the gods had left satisfied, and you had traded nothing of substance for a great deal of divine goodwill. The compact is intact. The seals hold. And in the south, doors are opening for you — quietly, without announcement — because something of Olympus has whispered your name favorably. You were not the strongest. You were the most careful. That's enough.
endings.devotion: You gave everything back. The names, the seals, the compact, the chest — all of it returned to whoever the gods agreed it rightfully belonged to, which turned out to be the dead themselves, which turned out to mean you released it all into the storm and watched it dissolve into the sea-spray like salt returning to salt. The keep is empty now. Lighter. You are lighter. The dead don't need a witness anymore because they've been witnessed fully — seen, named, acknowledged, released. You could leave. The roads south are passable. The war is truly over. But you find you don't want to. Glimthaven Keep without its burden is simply a house on a cliff above a gray sea, and that, you realize, is exactly what you've needed all along: a place that belongs to no one, that demands nothing, where you can finally rest.
